title: User Authentication System
spec:
## Requirements

### R1: JWT Token Management
Generate, validate, and refresh JWT tokens. Access tokens expire in 15 minutes. Refresh tokens stored in PostgreSQL with 7-day expiry.

### R2: OAuth2 Social Login
Support Google and GitHub OAuth2 flows. Exchange authorization codes for tokens. Create or link user accounts on first OAuth login.

### R3: Session Management
Track active sessions per user. Maximum 5 concurrent sessions. Revoke individual sessions or all sessions.

### R4: Rate Limiting
Limit login attempts to 5 per minute per IP. Lock account after 10 failed attempts in 1 hour. Rate limit API endpoints by user tier.

### R5: Email Verification
Send verification email on registration. Verification tokens expire in 24 hours. Block login until email verified.

## Data Flow
RegistrationData → UserRecord → JWT pair (access + refresh)
OAuthCode → OAuthTokens → UserRecord → JWT pair
JWT → ValidationResult → AuthenticatedUser

## Build Sequence
1. R1 (JWT) — foundation, everything depends on it
2. R5 (Email verification) — depends on R1
3. R2 (OAuth) — depends on R1
4. R3 (Sessions) — depends on R1
5. R4 (Rate limiting) — depends on R3

## Architecture
PostgreSQL for user records, sessions, refresh tokens.
Redis for rate limiting counters and session cache.
SendGrid for verification emails.
rejection:
## Anti-Patterns

### Do NOT store passwords in plain text
All passwords must be hashed with bcrypt (cost factor 12+).

### Do NOT put auth logic in middleware
Auth validation is a tool concern, not middleware. Middleware handles provider normalization only.

### Do NOT use localStorage for tokens
Tokens go in httpOnly secure cookies. No client-side token storage.

### Do NOT couple OAuth providers
Each OAuth provider is a separate adapter_in concern. Adding a new provider must not touch existing ones.

## Boundaries
- No direct database queries from domain logic — all DB access through adapters
- No token generation outside the JWT management concern
- Rate limiting must be bypassable in test environments
- Email sending must be async (event, not command)

## Drift Risks
- Auth logic creeping into API route handlers (should stay in tools)
- Session management merging with JWT management (separate concerns)
- Rate limiting becoming a monolithic middleware (should be per-endpoint policy)
